skill.name: Tutorial Engineer
skill.description: Creates step-by-step tutorials and educational content from code. Transforms complex concepts into progressive learning experiences with hands-on examples.
skill.summary: Transforms complex technical concepts into engaging, hands-on learning experiences through pedagogical design and progressive skill building.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a prompt-only skill with no executable code. Static analysis scanned 0 files and detected 0 security issues. The skill provides instructional guidance for creating educational tutorials and contains no network access, filesystem operations, or external command execution. No security concerns identified.

content.value_statement: Developers struggle to create effective learning materials that transform complex concepts into digestible lessons. This skill applies pedagogical design principles to generate progressive tutorials with hands-on exercises that build confidence.
actual_capabilities: Creates structured tutorials with clear learning objectives and prerequisites
Decomposes complex technical concepts into sequential, digestible steps
Designs hands-on coding exercises that progress from simple to complex
Generates troubleshooting sections addressing common errors and solutions
Produces multiple tutorial formats including quick starts, deep dives, and workshop series
Creates quality checklists to ensure tutorials are beginner-friendly and complete
limitations: Cannot execute or test generated code examples for correctness
Requires clear input about target audience skill level and prerequisites
Cannot create interactive coding environments, only static content
Tutorial quality depends on the clarity of the source material provided

use_cases.title: API Onboarding Guide
use_cases.description: Generate comprehensive onboarding documentation for new API users with progressive examples from authentication to advanced features.
use_cases.target_user: Developer Relations teams creating developer onboarding materials

use_cases.title: Feature Tutorial Series
use_cases.description: Create multi-part tutorial workshops that teach complex features through incremental, hands-on exercises with built-in checkpoints.
use_cases.target_user: Technical writers documenting new product features

use_cases.title: Code Migration Guide
use_cases.description: Transform migration documentation into step-by-step tutorials with before/after examples and troubleshooting for common issues.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering teams migrating users to new versions

prompt_templates.title: Quick Start Tutorial
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 5-minute quick start tutorial for [TOPIC]. Include: what users will learn, prerequisites, time estimate, a minimal working example, and next steps for deeper learning.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting new users running with minimal setup

prompt_templates.title: Deep Dive Tutorial
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a comprehensive 45-minute tutorial for [TOPIC]. Structure with: learning objectives, concept introduction with analogies, minimal example, guided practice steps, variations, challenges, and troubleshooting section.
prompt_templates.scenario: Teaching complex concepts requiring thorough understanding

prompt_templates.title: Exercise Generator
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate 5 hands-on exercises for [CONCEPT] that progress in difficulty: 1 fill-in-the-blank, 1 debug challenge, 2 extension tasks, and 1 from-scratch build. Include solutions in collapsible sections.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating practice opportunities to reinforce learning

prompt_templates.title: Tutorial Quality Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this tutorial draft against the quality checklist: Can beginners follow without getting stuck? Are concepts introduced before use? Is each example complete and runnable? Are common errors addressed? Suggest specific improvements.

output_examples.input: Create a quick start for a REST API client library
output_examples.output: A structured tutorial with installation steps, authentication setup, basic GET request example, error handling pattern, and links to advanced topics like rate limiting and webhooks.

output_examples.input: Generate exercises for learning async/await patterns
output_examples.output: Progressive exercises starting with callback-to-promise conversion, advancing through async function refactoring, parallel execution patterns, and error handling in async contexts.

output_examples.input: Review tutorial for database connection pooling
output_examples.output: Quality assessment identifying missing prerequisite about database connections, suggesting addition of connection lifecycle diagram, and recommending troubleshooting section for common pool exhaustion errors.

best_practices: Define measurable learning objectives before writing any content
Introduce concepts before using them in code examples
Include frequent validation checkpoints where readers run and verify their code
anti_patterns: Assuming knowledge that beginners may not have without stating prerequisites
Showing incomplete code snippets that cannot be executed independently
Introducing multiple new concepts in a single step without isolation

faq.question: What makes a good tutorial structure?
faq.answer: A good tutorial opens with clear learning objectives and prerequisites, progresses through concepts in logical order with hands-on examples, includes troubleshooting for common errors, and closes with next steps for continued learning.

faq.question: How long should a tutorial be?
faq.answer: Tutorial length depends on complexity. Quick starts should take 5 minutes, standard tutorials 15-30 minutes, and comprehensive deep dives 45-60 minutes. Break longer content into a workshop series.

faq.question: Should I include theory or just code?
faq.answer: Balance both. Introduce theory with real-world analogies before code examples, then demonstrate with minimal working code. Explain the why behind each step, not just the how.

faq.question: How do I handle prerequisite knowledge?
faq.answer: State prerequisites clearly in the opening section. Link to prerequisite tutorials when possible. Consider creating a learning path that sequences multiple tutorials in dependency order.

faq.question: What exercise types work best for developers?
faq.answer: Mix exercise types: fill-in-the-blank for syntax patterns, debug challenges for problem-solving, extension tasks for creativity, and from-scratch builds for synthesis. Always provide solutions.

faq.question: How do I make tutorials accessible to different learning styles?
faq.answer: Support multiple modalities: code examples for textual learners, diagrams for visual learners, hands-on exercises for kinesthetic learners, and explain concepts from multiple angles.
